paragraphs.body: Cross-cultural adaptation and belonging: How to promote the integration of Chinese students into foreign universities through two-way interaction

annotations.comment: Please move the introduction to the second page of the draft and add page numbers to the draft.

paragraphs.body: With the accelerated development of globalization, the internationalization of education has become an important trend in developing colleges and universities. As a major exporter of international students, the adaptation of Chinese students in overseas universities has attracted wide attention. According to the Open Doors Report, Chinese students account for over 30% of international students (Ching et al., 2017). However, influenced by cultural differences, language barriers, and inadequate social support, Chinese students face challenges such as loneliness, cultural conflict, and psychological stress during the adjustment process (Du & Wei, 2015), which not only affects academic performance but may also harm mental health.

Studies have shown that social connection and belonging are important factors in promoting international students' adaptation, including mainstream social connection and ethnic community connection (Thomas & Zhou, 2019). Based on this, I will analyze how to improve the cross-cultural adaptability of Chinese students through two-way student-institution efforts and propose specific solutions to enhance the sense of belonging, thereby improving mental health and academic performance. Cross-cultural adaptation challenges for Chinese students [Student-level challenges] Chinese students face many adaptive challenges during their study abroad experience. First, I think the language barrier is a universal problem. Inadequate language ability will limit students' ability to integrate into mainstream society and lead to increased self-isolation (Hu & Charles, 2013). For example, Chinese students often feel powerless in classroom discussions and academic writing because of the difficulty of expression and lack of grounding, which reduces their sense of participation and self-confidence. Meanwhile, cultural conflict is another big problem for Chinese students. The collectivist cultural background of Chinese students contrasts with the culture of many Western countries that emphasizes individualism, which is likely to lead to misunderstandings or difficulties in adaptation (Ching et al., 2017). For example, in Western classrooms, students are encouraged to take the initiative to ask questions and participate in discussions, while Chinese students tend to remain humble. In my opinion, this difference may affect students' interaction with teachers and peers and lead to misunderstandings. In addition, psychological stress and a lack of social support systems are also important challenges. Lack of a sense of belonging will lead to increased isolation of students, resulting in anxiety and depression (Chen & Zhou, 2019). I think this mental health issue further affects students' academic performance and social adjustment.

annotations.comment: Please format the headings according to APA.

paragraphs.body: [Challenges at the institutional level] I believe there are shortcomings in institutional policies and practices regarding the acceptance of international students. Although many universities have international student offices and provide some international student services, the support services offered are often not targeted and continuous (Tsegay et al., 2018). For example, the orientation activities of most institutions focus only on the initial adjustment of students and ignore their long-term needs throughout their studies. In addition, due to limited resources, many colleges and universities fail to make full use of cross-cultural training and community building to help international students adapt to the mainstream culture, which makes some students rely on ethnic communities, which further aggravates the gap between them and the mainstream society (Du & Wei, 2015). So, I think there is a lack of support for the acceptance of international students, especially in terms of meeting long-term needs and helping with cross-cultural adaptation. At the same time, orientation activities often focus only on initial adaptation, ignoring follow-up support, limited resources and inadequate community building further hindering international students' integration into mainstream culture.

annotations.comment: This is a run-on sentence. I would start a new sentence here.
I would start a new sentence here as as to avoid mentioning "which" twice in the sentence.

paragraphs.body: The critical role of social connection and belonging In my opinion, the adaptation of international students is not only dependent on the policy support of colleges and universities but also closely related to their own performance in social connection. While inadequate support from institutions may lead international students to rely more on ethnic communities, this does not mean that mainstream social connections and ethnic community connections are antithetical. Rather, these two connections need to complement each other in the adjustment process of international students to achieve the dual goals of mental health and social inclusion.

[Multi-dimensional social connectivity] Social connection, including Mainstream SC and Ethnic SC, is an important mechanism to facilitate Chinese students' adaptation. Mainstream social connectivity can enhance students' life satisfaction and positive emotions through interaction with local classmates and communities (Du & Wei, 2015). For example, by participating in campus activities or group projects, Chinese students can integrate into mainstream culture more quickly while building new social networks. On the other hand, ethnic community connection provides students with cultural identity and emotional support (Thomas & Zhou, 2019). This connection helps students find a sense of belonging in a foreign land through fellow citizens, friends, and cultural activities. For example, many Chinese students feel a sense of familiarity and cultural pride through participation in Chinese language societies or Spring Festival activities. Therefore, I think this balance of dual connections plays a key role in students' mental health and resilience. By balancing mainstream social connections with ethnic community connections, students not only receive psychological support but also find a sense of belonging in campus life, thereby further enhancing their resilience and academic performance. Because a sense of belonging is not only an important foundation for mental health but also a key factor for academic success. Students who lack a sense of belonging are more likely to feel isolated and stressed, which in turn affects their academic performance (Tsegay et al., 2018). By enhancing students' sense of belonging to the campus community, they can effectively relieve their psychological pressure in the process of adaptation and improve life satisfaction and academic performance (Ching et al., 2017). This made me realize that the establishment of a sense of belonging does not only depend on individual efforts but also needs the positive shaping of the campus environment. If institutions focus more on fostering a sense of belonging in their policies and practices, they will not only help international students overcome the challenges of adaptation but also inject more diversity and inclusion into their campuses. Two-way interactive solutions To achieve the win-win effect of student adaptation and campus inclusiveness, it is necessary for students to actively interact with the support measures of colleges and universities to jointly promote the establishment of a sense of belonging and the improvement of adaptability.

annotations.comment: This paragraph is a little long. I would start a new paragraph here.

paragraphs.body: [Student level effort] Students' active adaptation is significant for problem-solving. First, Chinese students can expand their social network by actively participating in campus activities, cultural festivals, and academic seminars (Hu & Charles, 2013). Secondly, students can also find language partners or tutors through online platforms or on-campus resources for more support and guidance. In addition, mental health self-management is also very important. For example, students can regularly contact family or friends to share their feelings; Or seek help through the psychological counseling service on campus (Thomas & Zhou, 2019). These measures can help relieve their stress and boost their confidence.

annotations.text: to share their feelings; Or seek
annotations.comment: to share their feelings or seek help...

paragraphs.body: [Support at the institutional level] Institutions should adopt more inclusive and diverse policies to accommodate international students. Cross-cultural training programs can help local students and staff better understand the needs and cultural backgrounds of international students (Tsegay et al., 2018). In addition, colleges and universities should establish "international student mentor programs" to pair freshmen with upperclassmen and help them adapt to campus life more quickly (Thomas & Zhou, 2019). Through partnerships with ethnic communities, such as Chinese language festivals, institutions can inject diversity and vitality into their campuses while promoting a sense of belonging among international students (Du & Wei, 2015). Conclusion I think the adjustment process for Chinese students in foreign universities is challenging but also a journey with great potential. Through a two-way effort between students and institutions, their sense of belonging, mental health, and academic performance can be significantly enhanced. At the same time, I analyze the key role of social connection and belonging in the adaptation process and propose a feasible solution to the adaptation problem through two-way interaction. It is believed that soon, the field of international education will become more inclusive and supportive, helping every Chinese student not only overcome the challenges in the process of adaptation, but also fully tap their potential, integrate into the new culture and environment, and achieve personal growth and academic success. Reference 1. Ching, Y., Renes, S. L., McMurrow, S., Simpson, J., & Strange, A. T.
